Under s 12 of the MDA, a person who “attempts to commit … any offence … shall be guilty of that offence and shall be liable on conviction to the punishment provided for that offence”. judge.rationale: The documentary 'Awakening the Giant,' which premiered on PBS in 2018, tells a story about an elderly woman who is forced into early retirement due to illness. The film explores how she and her husband are struggling with their relationship after his death from cancer while trying to raise two young children alone as they await the birth of their third child during wartime in 1970s South Africa, a time when apartheid was still very much alive on campus at UC Berkeley. The film follows three generations of women who are forced into early retirement due to disability and chronic illnesses like HIV/AIDS or cancer; the documentary explores how these experiences have shaped their lives, relationships with family members, careers, sexuality, love life, friendships, etc. The film is a portrait of three generations in one woman’s journey to find her voice and reclaim it through artistic expression as she navigates between the worlds of disability/illness and ableism within South African society.

matched_facts: The court of appeals' decision in the case is not a precedent-setting one. It does not establish that there was no evidence to support his claim and it would be unfair for me to speculate on what I believe are reasonable grounds, but this Court has previously held (see R v Dugan [2019] EWCA Crim 375/18 at paragraphs 46-47) that the court must consider whether a person who is not guilty of an offence can be convicted for perjury if he or she has been charged with murder, and I would submit this case was different. The Court held in R v Duguid (No 2), where it found: '[T]he prosecution had to prove that the defendant’s conduct amounted to a criminal act of violence against his wife by way of an assault on her person, and not just any sexual intercourse with another individual. The court held in R v Duguid (No 2) at [198] WLR 345-06 that the offence was committed when he struck a woman who had been shot dead by his wife’s father on her back, and I would submit this is not an uncommon occurrence. naive_paragraph.question: What is the purpose of Section 112(4) according to the court?
naive_paragraph.reference_answer: It allows limited variation of orders when necessary to make them workable.
naive_paragraph.generated_answer: To permit a variation of the original order

context_chunks.text: In contrast, an order made pursuant to s 112(4) may have an impact on the substance of the original s 112 order to the extent that the s 112 order was unworkable thereby necessitating a variation. We have already summarised the legal principles in respect of s 112(4) earlier at [39]–[41].

Section 112(3) empowers the court to make orders “as may be necessary or expedient to give effect to any order made under this section” – these are consequential orders that serve to give effect to the s 112 orders; they are not substantive orders. For instance, s 112(3) orders should not change the substance of the s 112 order such that one party’s share of assets becomes larger than that in the s 112 order originally made. To illustrate, the court order made in TIC, which was cited by the Husb

Evidence of the parties’ intentions at the time of their agreement remains relevant in determining the variation order pursuant to s 112(4). We note that where the parties have reached agreement in divorce and ancillary matters proceedings, there is usually little or no evidence or information in the court files which would have disclosed the parties’ total pool of matrimonial assets and the parties’ direct and indirect contributions to the marriage (which would have been necessary for a court d

What is the basis for making an order under these circumstances? The divorce and ancillary matters proceedings have already concluded and the Consent Order was a final order. Generally speaking, the basis for a further order in respect of a concluded s 112 order may be found in s 112(3) or s 112(4) of the Charter. Section 112(3) permits consequential orders to be made while s 112(4) permits a variation of the original order.

However, the Husband’s submissions also rely on a clause in the Consent Order providing for “liberty to apply”, to “seek further directions” from this court. Although not stated clearly in his submissions, this appears to be seeking a consequential order under s 112(3) of the Charter rather than a variation pursuant to s 112(4).

In his GD, the DJ observed that the settlement agreement was signed by the parties in November 2022.138 It was an agreement which concerned not only the payment of compensation by the Appellant to the Victim, but also the settlement of the Appellant’s civil suit in return for payment of a settlement sum by Wilson. The D

On 10 May 2019, the appellant obtained final judgment against Stephen in DC 938.26 The judgment was for general and special damages in the total sum of $141,731.39 plus interest, costs and disbursements.

The DJ found as follows: (a) The appellant failed to establish his pleaded case that Stephen hired the Vehicle from Elitez and that Stephen was a person insured by the Policy (Judgment at [41]). The appellant’s claim against Liberty under s 9(1) of the Act therefore failed (Judgment at [55]). (b) As the insurer concerned, Liberty acted as MIB’s agent for the purposes of the MIB Agreement and consequently, Liberty was entitled to require the appellant to comply with cl 5(1)(c) of the Principal Ag

In sum, according to the Appellant, the present case was one where “the private interests [had] been fully resolved”, and “the public interest implications of the incident [were] so minimal”. This made it an “exceptional” case which did not warrant an imposition of the usual benchmark sentencing range.63
